title: Chapter 3: Prove Your Worth
content: The Liang family's eastern shipping operation was bleeding money like a gutted fish.

Ren learned this the way he learned everything in this house—by being invisible in a room full of people who refused to see him. The family office occupied the estate's east wing, a sterile maze of glass walls and hushed conversations where Liang Xue held court like a queen in a documentary about collapsing empires.

He'd come to return a book—something he'd grabbed from the library to maintain his cover as the shiftless son-in-law who browsed to avoid work. But the door to Xue's private office was ajar, and he could hear her voice, taut as a bowstring.
"—I don't care what the harbor authority says. Those containers were sealed when they left Singapore. If the customs inspection shows tampering, someone on *our* end facilitated it."
A pause. Then, quieter: "No. Don't involve my uncle. I'll handle this myself."

Ren set the book on the hallway table and walked away. The War God in him had already mapped the problem: three shipping containers of pharmaceutical-grade compounding agents, rerouted through a port authority controlled by the Zhao conglomerate. The Zhao family had been circling Liang Corp for two years, picking at the edges, testing for weakness. This wasn't theft—it was sabotage. Replace the sealed cargo with tainted product, let the quality inspection catch it, and suddenly the Liang family's pharmaceutical division loses its government contract.

Classic corporate warfare. The kind of thing Ren had seen a thousand times in the previous timeline, when corporations had operated like city-states and supply chains were the front lines of undeclared wars.
The solution was obvious to him. It would have been obvious to anyone with his experience—which was to say, obvious to approximately no one on this planet.
The problem was presenting it without explaining how he knew.

He spent the afternoon in the estate's library—three floors of floor-to-ceiling shelves, leather chairs older than the republic, and a fireplace that probably cost more than his childhood home. The librarian, a white-haired woman named Auntie Mei, had stopped asking why the useless son-in-law spent his days reading logistics manuals and customs law. She just brought him tea and left him alone.
By evening, he had a plan.

There were exactly two people in the Liang household who might listen to him without immediate contempt. One was Auntie Mei, and she wasn't in the power structure. The other was Liang Xue's executive assistant, a round-faced young man named Sun Wei who had the survival instincts of a cockroach and the organizational skills of a god.
Ren caught him in the service corridor at seven p.m., when the staff was changing shifts and the halls were empty.
"Sun Wei."
The assistant nearly jumped out of his skin. "Mr. Zhou! You— I didn't hear you—"
"I need you to pass a message to your boss."
Sun Wei's expression cycled through fear, confusion, and the calculating look of a man deciding whether this conversation would get him fired. "Mrs. Liang doesn't take messages from—"

"Tell her: the Zhao family has a mole in the Eastern Harbor customs office. Name of Director Wu. He's been on their payroll for eighteen months. The tainted containers were pre-sealed at the Zhao warehouse in Shenzhen and swapped during the Singapore layover using forged bills of lading. The serial numbers won't match because the originals were filed under a shell company—Constantine Logistics, registered in the Caymans. Cross-reference the cargo manifest's declared weight against the port's weighbridge records. The discrepancy will prove the swap."
Sun Wei stared.

"Also," Ren added, "Director Wu's daughter is engaged to a Zhao family member. Third cousin. It's how they recruited him. He didn't do it for money—he did it for his daughter's wedding. Which means he'll crack if you approach it right. Don't go to the police. Go to him directly. Offer him a retirement package and a guarantee that his daughter's engagement won't be affected. He'll give up the whole network."
"How do you—"
"Does it matter?" Ren held the assistant's gaze. "Does *how I know* matter more than the information being correct?"
Sun Wei swallowed hard. Then he nodded once and disappeared down the corridor.
The next morning, Liang Xue's shipping crisis resolved itself with surgical precision.

Director Wu was quietly retired. Constantine Logistics was exposed as a Zhao front. The customs investigation shifted from "Liang quality control failure" to " Zhao corporate espionage," and the government contract was not only preserved but expanded on the strength of Xue's "proactive security measures."
The Liang family's stock rose 3% by noon.

Ren heard about it from Auntie Mei, who heard it from the kitchen staff, who heard it from the drivers, who heard it from the guards. Gossip in the Liang estate was a precision instrument—faster and more accurate than any corporate intelligence network.
He was in the library, pretending to read a history of maritime law, when the door opened.
Liang Xue walked in.

She wasn't in her usual corporate armor—no blazer, no heels. Instead, she wore a cream cardigan over a simple blouse, and her hair was down, falling past her shoulders in a black waterfall. Without the severity of her public persona, she looked younger. Softer.
Dangerous in an entirely different way.
"Sun Wei told me." She stopped ten feet away, arms folded, studying Ren like he was a specimen she couldn't quite categorize. "How did you know about Director Wu?"
"Does it matter?"
"It matters to me."
Ren closed the book. "I read. I observe. I connect dots that other people don't see."
"You connected those dots from *this library*?" Skepticism dripped from every syllable.

"The information was all public. Director Wu's daughter's engagement was in the social pages. Constantine Logistics was in the Caymans registry. The weight discrepancy is standard forensic accounting. None of it required special access—just the willingness to look."
"Why would you look?"

The question hung in the air between them. Ren could feel the Salvation System monitoring her biometrics—elevated heart rate, dilated pupils, micro-expressions cycling too fast for normal processing. She wasn't just curious. She was *alarmed*.

Because he'd solved in an afternoon what her entire team had failed to catch in a week. Because he'd done it from a library with no resources but his own mind. Because the useless son-in-law she'd married out of family obligation had just demonstrated a strategic capability that had no business existing in a broke, powerless, supposedly worthless man.
"I told you at the banquet," Ren said quietly. "I'm not signing those papers. And I'm not useless."
Liang Xue's jaw tightened. For a long moment, she said nothing. Then she turned and walked toward the door.
At the threshold, she paused.
"The next time you have information that could save this family a quarter-billion yuan..." She didn't turn around. "...bring it to me directly. Not through my assistant."
She left.
The Salvation System pulsed:

`CAUSAL BACKDOOR STATUS: Reinforced`
`SUBJECT TRUST METRIC: 12% → 18%`
`NOTE: Trust increment significant. Subject re-evaluating baseline assumptions.`
Eighteen percent. Not enough to survive on. But it was a start.
Ren allowed himself a thin smile. In the war against the Abyss, he'd been a soldier—brute force, direct confrontation, attrition warfare that ground both sides to dust.
Here, in this gilded cage of marble and malice, he was something else entirely.
He was a spy. And the enemy didn't even know they were at war.
His phone buzzed. The unknown number again.
*SPEAKER: "Impressive, Mr. Zhou. But the Liang family's shipping problems are small concerns. Would you like to discuss what's really happening beneath that estate?"*
The line went dead before he could respond.
Ren looked at the phone. Then at the ceiling above him—beyond which, somewhere in the restricted inner sanctum, the dimensional array was slowly drinking power from the earth's leylines.
Two players had approached him in three days. One through a phone call, one through the system. Both offering information. Both with their own agendas.
*In the Abyss War,* Shen Wei had written in her final briefing, *the hardest battles weren't against the enemy. They were figuring out who the enemy was.*
One thousand ninety-three days.
